revision.text: #redirect[[en:Ōji-no-Kami]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

== 王子信仰 == 日本には古来、本宮と呼ばれる[[神社]]の主神からその子供の神として分かれ出た神格を祀ったり、巫女的な性格をもつ母神とその子神をあわせて祀る信仰があり、これを若宮<small>（わかみや）</small>あるいは御子神<small>（みこがみ）</small>と呼んでいた。のちに仏教と[[神道]]の[[神仏習合|習合]]が進むと、[[仏教]]の神格のひとつで、図像では仏に顧従する[[児童]]の姿で表現される「[[童子]]」が若宮と習合され、王子と呼ばれた。また、王子をまつる社<small>（やしろ）</small>も王子とよばれることがある。
このような習合が顕著だったのは[[熊野信仰|熊野権現信仰]]においてであり、[[平安時代]]の末期に[[熊野十二所権現]]のうちの五柱である五所王子と呼ばれる神々が信仰されるようになった。そのひとつである若王子<small>（にゃくおうじ）</small>あるいは[[若一王子]]<small>（にゃくいちおうじ）</small>は少年あるいは少女の姿であらわされる神で、全国の熊野信仰において熊野権現を[[勧請]]する際に、多くの場合この神が祀られた。
また同じ時期には[[祇園神社|祇園社]]の[[牛頭天王]]や[[日吉大社]]の[[山王権現]]の[[眷属神]]として王子の姿をした八柱の神格である[[八王子神社|八王子権現]]があらわれ、病気を払う力をもった霊威あらたかな神として広く信仰された。
== 地名の王子 == 王子信仰の隆盛とともに、王子信仰の社がある場所が王子の地名で呼ばれることが増え、王子の地名が全国に広がった。
[[東京都]][[北区 (東京都)|北区]]の[[王子 (東京都北区)|王子]]もその一つで、若王子権現の社（現[[王子神社]]）があったことからついた地名である。また同じく東京の[[八王子市|八王子]]は、地名の起こりである[[八王子城]]が八王子権現の社（現在の八王子神社）がまつられた山に築城されたことに由来している。
ただし、王子の地名は「窪んだ土地」を意味する「おほち（おおち、[[島根県]]の[[邑智郡]]など）」あるいは「あふち（おうち、凹地）」に由来しているものもあるという説もある。それによれば、窪地であるから「おおち」と呼ばれた、あるいは高いところから窪んで急に落ち込んだ坂のことを「おおちの坂」と呼んだことからついた地名である。先述の王子の場合、[[石神井川]]に面した丘陵の上にあり、南の[[滝野川 (東京都北区)|滝野川]]地区に向かって急な坂となっている。

{{出典の明記|date=2011年8月}} '''王子神'''（おうじがみ）は、日本の神の一つ。単に'''王子'''とも呼ばれ、その信仰を'''王子信仰'''という。
== 王子信仰 == 日本には古来、本宮と呼ばれる[[神社]]の主神からその子供の神として分かれ出た神格を祀ったり、[[巫女]]的な性格をもつ母神とその子神をあわせて祀る信仰があり、これを若宮<small>（わかみや）</small>あるいは[[御子神]]<small>（みこがみ）</small>と呼んでいた。のちに[[仏教]]と[[神道]]の[[神仏習合|習合]]が進むと、仏教の神格のひとつで、図像では仏に顧従する[[児童]]の姿で表現される「[[童子]]」が若宮と習合され、王子と呼ばれた。また、王子をまつる社<small>（やしろ）</small>も王子と呼ばれることがある。
このような習合が顕著だったのは[[熊野信仰|熊野権現信仰]]においてであり、[[平安時代]]の末期に[[熊野十二所権現]]のうちの五柱である五所王子と呼ばれる神々が信仰されるようになった。そのひとつである若王子<small>（にゃくおうじ）</small>あるいは[[若一王子]]<small>（にゃくいちおうじ）</small>は少年あるいは少女の姿であらわされる神で、全国の熊野信仰において熊野権現を[[勧請]]する際に、多くの場合この神が祀られた。
また同じ時期には、[[祇園神社|祇園社]]の[[牛頭天王]]や[[日吉大社]]の[[山王権現]]の眷属神として、王子の姿をした八柱の神格である[[八王子神社|八王子権現]]があらわれ、病気を払う力をもった霊威あらたかな神として広く信仰された。
== 地名の王子 == 王子信仰の隆盛とともに、王子信仰の社がある場所が王子の地名で呼ばれることが増え、王子の地名が全国に広がった。
[[東京都]][[北区 (東京都)|北区]]の[[王子 (東京都北区)|王子]]もその一つで、若王子権現の社（現[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]）があったことからついた地名である。また同じく東京の[[八王子市|八王子]]は、地名の起こりである[[八王子城]]が八王子権現の社（現在の八王子神社）が祀られた山に築城されたことに由来している。
ただし、王子の地名は「窪んだ土地」を意味する「おほち（おおち、[[島根県]]の[[邑智郡]]など）」あるいは「あふち（おうち、凹地）」に由来しているものもあるという説もある。それによれば、窪地であるから「おおち」と呼ばれた、あるいは高いところから窪んで急に落ち込んだ坂のことを「おおちの坂」と呼んだことからついた地名である。先述の王子の場合、[[石神井川]]に面した丘陵の上にあり、南の[[滝野川 (東京都北区)|滝野川]]地区に向かって急な坂となっている。
== 関連項目 == * [[御子神]] * [[若一王子]] * [[王子 (東京都北区)]] * [[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]] * [[九十九王子]]
{{デフォルトソート:おうしかみ}} [[Category:修験道の神]] {{shinto-stub}}{{Buddhism-stub}}

ただし、王子の地名は「窪んだ土地」を意味する「おほち（おおち、[[島根県]]の[[邑智郡]]など）」あるいは「あふち（おうち、凹地）」に由来しているものもあるという説もある。それによれば、窪地であるから「おおち」と呼ばれた、あるいは高いところから窪んで急に落ち込んだ坂のことを「おおちの坂」と呼んだことからついた地名である。先述の王子の場合、[[石神井川]]に面した丘陵の上にあり、南の[[滝野川 (東京都北区)|滝野川]]地区に向かって急な坂となっている。

revision.text:
{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Twelve Deities|ja|熊野十二所権現}}, known as the {{ill|Five Goshō Ōji|ja|五所王子}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}

For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Twelve Deities|ja|熊野十二所権現}}, known as the {{ill|Five Goshō Ōji|ja|五所王子}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Twelve Deities|ja|熊野十二所権現}}, known as the {{ill|Five Goshō Ōji|ja|五所王子}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.

Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Twelve Deities|ja|熊野十二所権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}}

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.

Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Ōji-no-Kami—>[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:王子神]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]<!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:Sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:王子神]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]]

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[category:qq close]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]
[[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] [[ja:王子神]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]]

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]

{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}.
== See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]

revision.text:
{{Source attribution|date=2011年8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}. == See Also! == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category: tier 1]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]
[[Category:11]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] [[ja:王子神]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}} [[Category:New]]

{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|ja|若宮}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|ja|牛頭天王|1=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|ja|日吉大社}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} in {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji, Tokyo|ja|八王子市}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|ja|八王子城}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|ja|石神井川}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社}} * {{ill|Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji}} {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:to be resorted]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:to be resorted]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]] <!--Ōji-no-Kami-->[[Category:11]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Ōji-no-Kami}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Shugendō deities]] {{citation needed|Oji-no-Kami}} [[Category:Kumano faith]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ōji-no-Kami]][[Category:Ōji]] [[Category:kk]]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2011年8月}} '''王子神'''（おうじがみ）は、日本の神の一つ。単に'''王子'''とも呼ばれ、その信仰を'''王子信仰'''という。
== 王子信仰 == 日本には古来、本宮と呼ばれる[[神社]]の主神からその子供の神として分かれ出た神格を祀ったり、[[巫女]]的な性格をもつ母神とその子神をあわせて祀る信仰があり、これを若宮<small>（わかみや）</small>あるいは[[御子神]]<small>（みこがみ）</small>と呼んでいた。のちに[[仏教]]と[[神道]]の[[神仏習合|習合]]が進むと、仏教の神格のひとつで、図像では仏に顧従する[[児童]]の姿で表現される「[[童子]]」が若宮と習合され、王子と呼ばれた。また、王子をまつる社<small>（やしろ）</small>も王子と呼ばれることがある。
このような習合が顕著だったのは[[熊野信仰|熊野権現信仰]]においてであり、[[平安時代]]の末期に[[熊野十二所権現]]のうちの五柱である五所王子と呼ばれる神々が信仰されるようになった。そのひとつである若王子<small>（にゃくおうじ）</small>あるいは[[若一王子]]<small>（にゃくいちおうじ）</small>は少年あるいは少女の姿であらわされる神で、全国の熊野信仰において熊野権現を[[勧請]]する際に、多くの場合この神が祀られた。
また同じ時期には、[[祇園神社|祇園社]]の[[牛頭天王]]や[[日吉大社]]の[[山王権現]]の眷属神として、王子の姿をした八柱の神格である[[八王子神社|八王子権現]]があらわれ、病気を払う力をもった霊威あらたかな神として広く信仰された。
== 地名の王子 == 王子信仰の隆盛とともに、王子信仰の社がある場所が王子の地名で呼ばれることが増え、王子の地名が全国に広がった。
[[東京都]][[北区 (東京都)|北区]]の[[王子 (東京都北区)|王子]]もその一つで、若王子権現の社（現[[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]]）があったことからついた地名である。また同じく東京の[[八王子市|八王子]]は、地名の起こりである[[八王子城]]が八王子権現の社（現在の八王子神社）が祀られた山に築城されたことに由来している。
ただし、王子の地名は「窪んだ土地」を意味する「おほち（おおち、[[島根県]]の[[邑智郡]]など）」あるいは「あふち（おうち、凹地）」に由来しているものもあるという説もある。それによれば、窪地であるから「おおち」と呼ばれた、あるいは高いところから窪んで急に落ち込んだ坂のことを「おおちの坂」と呼んだことからついた地名である。先述の王子の場合、[[石神井川]]に面した丘陵の上にあり、南の[[滝野川 (東京都北区)|滝野川]]地区に向かって急な坂となっている。
== 関連項目 == * [[御子神]] * [[若一王子]] * [[王子 (東京都北区)]] * [[王子神社 (東京都北区)|王子神社]] * [[九十九王子]]
{{デフォルトソート:おうしかみ}} [[Category:修験道の神]] [[Category:熊野信仰]] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|qq=|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|qid=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|qq=|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|qq=|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|qq=|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|qq=|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|qq=|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.
== See Also ==

For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.
