<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

* {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

* {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]] {{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11573000]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--Ōji-no-Kami--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:王子神]] [[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]][[sn:Ōji-no-Kami]]
{{wikidata link|Q11573000}} [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Has wikidata]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Shugendō deities]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Ōji]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]] {{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11573000]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}} '''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.
== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as [[:en:Wakamiya|Wakamiya]] or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]], the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.
During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s [[:en:Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tennō]] and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at [[:en:Hiyoshi Taisha|Hiyoshi Taisha]]. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.
== Ōji in Place Names == With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.
One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of [[:en:Hachiōji|Hachiōji, Tokyo]] originates from [[:en:Hachiōji Castle|Hachiōji Castle]], built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the [[:en:Shakujii River|Shakujii River]] on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

== Ōji Faith == In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.
This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tokyo}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.
Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|en|Shakujii River|WD=Q7487093|lt=Shakujii River}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|en|Shakujii River|WD=Q7487093|lt=Shakujii River}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}. == See Also == * {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}} * {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} * {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
{{Infobox deity}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11573000]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrine|WD=Q11588442}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tokyo}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|en|Shakujii River|WD=Q7487093|lt=Shakujii River}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

* {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}

With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tokyo}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|en|Shakujii River|WD=Q7487093|lt=Shakujii River}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Takinogawa area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:王子神]][[zh:王子神]]
{{Infobox deity}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrines|WD=Q11588442}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tokyo|1=Hachiōji}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|en|Shakujii River|WD=Q7487093|lt=Shakujii River|1=Shakujii River}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Q11565310|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.
== See Also ==

* {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}
* {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrines|WD=Q11588442}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tokyo|1=Hachiōji}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|en|Shakujii River|WD=Q7487093|lt=Shakujii River|1=Shakujii River}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Q11565310|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.

{{Infobox deity}}
{{Source attribution|date=2011th year 8月}}
'''Ōji-no-Kami''' (王子神) is one of the deities in Japan. It is also simply referred to as '''Ōji''', and the faith surrounding it is called '''Ōji Shinkō'''.

== Ōji Faith ==
In ancient Japan, there was a belief of enshrining divine personas that branched off as child deities of the main deity at a shrine, referred to as {{ill|Wakamiya|en|Wakamiya|WD=Q100249640|lt=Wakamiya|1=Wakamiya}} or {{ill|Mikogami|ja|御子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikogami|qq=}}, often alongside maternal deities with shamanic characteristics. Over time, with the progression of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|WD=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, the Buddhist concept of {{ill|Dōji|ja|童子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōji|1=kumāra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598453}}, depicted as children attending Buddhas, merged with these young deities and came to be known as Ōji. Shrines dedicated to Ōji also adopted the name.

This syncretism was particularly prominent in the {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}}, where by the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, five deities of the {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野十二所権現|lt=Kumano Twelve Deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野権現}}, known as the {{ill|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja|五所王子|Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines=Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|lt=Five Goshō Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano_Ninety-Nine_Ōji#Five_Major_Ōji_Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3200280}}, gained prominence. Among these, the {{ill|Wakaōji|ja|若王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 若一王子|1=Nyakuichi Ōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616186}} or {{ill|Nyakuichi Ōji|ja|若一王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyakuichi Ōji|qq=}} deity, depicted as a boy or girl, was widely enshrined when {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}} was transferred elsewhere.

During the same era, eight deities in the form of Ōji, known as {{ill|Hachiōji Gongen|ja|八王子権現|1=Hachiōji Gongen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiōji Gongen|qq=}}, appeared as subordinate deities of the {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Gion shrines|WD=Q11588442}}'s {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|WD=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}} and the {{ill|Sannō Gongen|ja|山王権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sannō Gongen|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} at {{ill|Hiyoshi Taisha|en|Hiyoshi Taisha|WD=Q656451|lt=Hiyoshi Taisha|1=Hiyoshi Taisha}}. These deities were revered for their miraculous powers to ward off illnesses.

== Ōji in Place Names ==
With the rise of Ōji faith, locations housing shrines dedicated to Ōji came to bear the name, spreading such toponyms across Japan.

One example is {{ill|Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|ja|王子 (東京都北区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji, Kita-ku, Tokyo|1=Ōji, Kita, Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572961}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, named after the {{ill|Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja|若王子権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wakaōji Gongen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, now {{ill|Ōji Shrine|ja|王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōji Shrine|qq=}}. Similarly, the name of {{ill|Hachiōji|en|Hachiōji|WD=Q208863|lt=Hachiōji, Tokyo|1=Hachiōji}} originates from {{ill|Hachiōji Castle|en|Hachiōji Castle|WD=Q2969389|lt=Hachiōji Castle|1=Hachiōji Castle}}, built on a mountain enshrining the {{ill|Hachioji Shrine|ja|八王子神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachioji Shrine|qq=}}.

Alternatively, some scholars suggest that "Ōji" derives from words indicating "depression" in the terrain, such as "Ōchi" (窪地) or "Ōuchi" (凹地), which describe low-lying or steeply descending areas. For instance, the area of Ōji overlooks the {{ill|Shakujii River|en|Shakujii River|WD=Q7487093|lt=Shakujii River|1=Shakujii River}} on a hill, descending steeply towards the {{ill|Takinogawa area|ja|滝野川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takinogawa area|1=Q11565310|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11565310}}.
